serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one bowl), providing roughly 113 kcal, 16.3 g protein, and 3.0 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Seafood Coriander Soup in ICMR-NIN tables. Values estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for prawns, fish, squid, and coriander-based preparations, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual seafood components (shrimp, mixed fish, squid). The composite reflects a light clear soup with approximately 60–65% water content from broth dilution.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — analogous estimate for high-protein low-carbohydrate seafood soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Seafood Coriander Soup. GI estimated at 20 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood broth/soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 2.5 g per 100 g; GL per 250 g serving = (20 × 6.25 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.25, rounded to 1.3. High protein and minimal starch content strongly suppress glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 280 mg per 100 g (700 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size carefully, as a single 250 ml serving contributes approximately 700 mg sodium — about 30% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit intake to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~20) and low carbohydrate density (2.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seafood Coriander Soup is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of approximately 20, high lean protein content (6.5 g per 100 g), and minimal available carbohydrate, resulting in a glycemic load of only ~1.3 per serving, which supports stable postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean seafood protein, low saturated fat (0.3 g per 100 g), and anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The soup provides lean marine protein with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from prawns, fish, and squid; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the sodium content (280 mg per 100 g) and opt for low-salt preparation to avoid exceeding daily sodium targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from seafood and anti-inflammatory properties of coriander and ginger
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Seafood components provide omega-3 fatty acids that may help reduce joint inflammation; individuals with shellfish sensitivity should monitor personal tolerance and consult a clinician before regular consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Coriander Soup is a light, aromatic clear broth originating from the coastal regions of India. It is prepared by simmering a combination of fresh seafood — typically prawns, white fish fillets, and squid — in a spiced broth flavored with fresh coriander (cilantro), ginger, garlic, cumin, and black pepper, finished with a squeeze of lemon juice for brightness. The soup has a delicate, herbaceous character with subtle heat from the spices and a clean oceanic flavor from the seafood. The texture is thin and broth-forward, with tender pieces of seafood distributed throughout. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-calorie dish providing approximately 45 kcal per 100 g, 6.5 g protein, 1.2 g fat, and only 2.5 g carbohydrate, making it an excellent choice for weight management, post-exercise recovery, and clinical dietary plans. The very low glycemic index (~20) and minimal carbohydrate load make it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate at 280 mg per 100 g and should be monitored by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is considered halal. It is commonly served as a starter or light meal at lunch or dinner in coastal Indian cuisine.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Seafood Coriander Soup exists in ICMR-NIN published tables. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data for prawns, fish, and squid combined with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual seafood items. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from two approved primary sources is internally consistent and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite soup preparation is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific dish formulation; estimated water content is approximately 88–90% by weight based on broth dilution ratio but has not been confirmed by direct measurement in an approved source, and therefore -1 is used per G3 sentinel-value policy. Nutrient values are estimated composites from ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for prawns (USDA #15150), mixed fish, and squid (USDA #15149), adjusted for broth dilution at approximately 60% seafood-to-liquid ratio. Saturated fat (0.3 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for the mixed seafood components; these values carry medium confidence. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for fresh seafood and plant-based spice preparations per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Cholesterol estimated at 45 mg per 100 g based on USDA SR Legacy data for mixed shrimp and squid at the stated dilution ratio. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. Duplicate portion entries in the input (three identical 250 ml entries) have been deduplicated to a single default serving of 250 g. This dish is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual requirements for Chinese, Malay, and Tamil are not mandated by G6. Hindi local name is provided as the dish is of Indian origin. Chinese names (simplified and traditional) are provided as supplementary aliases for broader retrieval coverage.
